content_json.html: We are given a right-angled triangle as an independent object.
Squares can be built on the sides of this triangle.

In this construction, the triangle as an independent object disappears and becomes only the difference in the areas of the two squares. This creates a contradiction between the triangle as an independent object and the difference in the areas of the squares.

This contradiction is overcome in the form of the Pythagorean theorem, where the length of the hypotenuse is converted into an area (square) and then equated to the sum of the legs, which are also converted into areas.

This can be written as follows. There are two ways to find the area of a large square:

  1. 1.Sfirst method=(a+b)2=a2+2ab+b2S_{\text{first method}} = (a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2Sfirst method​=(a+b)2=a2+2ab+b2

  2. 2. Ssecond method=4(12⋅ab)+c2=2ab+c2S_{\text{second method}} = 4\left(\frac{1}{2} \cdot ab\right) + c^2 = 2ab + c^2Ssecond method​=4(21​⋅ab)+c2=2ab+c2

This gives:

a2+2ab+b2=2ab+c2a^2 + 2ab + b^2 = 2ab + c^2a2+2ab+b2=2ab+c2

Shorten it:

c2=a2+b2c^2 = a^2 + b^2c2=a2+b2
The Pythagorean theorem resolves the contradiction between a right triangle as an independent object and other geometric objects.
